title: Longest Chain of Words
description: Find longest chain where each word is subsequence of next

examples.explanation: Start: max_len=0, used=[F,F,F], chain=[]. Call backtrack([]). Chain empty, try all words. i=0: word="a", valid (chain empty), used[0]=T, chain=["a"]. Recurse backtrack(["a"]). Check unused: i=1 word="ab", is_subsequence("a","ab")=True, used[1]=T, chain=["a","ab"]. Recurse backtrack(["a","ab"]). Check unused: i=2 word="abc", is_subsequence("ab","abc")=True, used[2]=T, chain=["a","ab","abc"]. Recurse backtrack(["a","ab","abc"]). Update max_len=3. Backtrack: chain.pop to ["a","ab"], used[2]=F. No more unused, backtrack: chain.pop to ["a"], used[1]=F. i=2 word="abc", is_subsequence("a","abc")=True, used[2]=T, chain=["a","abc"]. Recurse backtrack(["a","abc"]). Check unused: i=1 word="ab", is_subsequence("abc","ab")=False (since 'c' missing), skip. Update max_len=2. Backtrack: chain.pop to ["a"], used[2]=F. Backtrack: chain.pop to [], used[0]=F. i=1: word="ab", valid, used[1]=T, chain=["ab"]. Recurse backtrack(["ab"]). Check unused: i=0 word="a", is_subsequence("ab","a")=False, skip. i=2 word="abc", valid, used[2]=T, chain=["ab","abc"]. Recurse, update max_len=2. Backtrack similarly. i=2: word="abc", valid, chain=["abc"], recurse, max_len=1. End: max_len=3.

Start: max_len=0, used=[F,F,F], chain=[]. Call backtrack([]). i=0: word="x", valid, used[0]=T, chain=["x"]. Recurse backtrack(["x"]). Check unused: i=1 word="xy", is_subsequence("x","xy")=True, used[1]=T, chain=["x","xy"]. Recurse, update max_len=2. Check i=2 word="yx", is_subsequence("x","yx")=True, used[2]=T, chain=["x","yx"]. Recurse, update max_len=2. Backtrack. i=1: word="xy", valid, chain=["xy"]. Recurse: i=0 word="x", is_subsequence("xy","x")=False; i=2 word="yx", is_subsequence("xy","yx")=False (order mismatch). Update max_len=1. i=2: word="yx", valid, chain=["yx"]. Recurse: i=0 word="x", is_subsequence("yx","x")=True, chain=["yx","x"], update max_len=2; i=1 word="xy", is_subsequence("yx","xy")=False. End: max_len=2.

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking O(n! * L)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations of words and check each chain for validity, where each word is a subsequence of the next. Use backtracking to explore all possible chains and track the longest valid one.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Chain of Words via Brute Force Backtracking
ladders.desc: Find the longest chain where each word is a subsequence of the next by exploring all permutations using backtracking.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this brute force approach is that we need to consider every possible ordering of words to find the longest valid chain. Since each word must be a subsequence of the next in the chain, we can't rely on simple sorting—we must test all permutations. Backtracking allows us to systematically explore all possible chains without storing all permutations at once, which would be memory-intensive. We start with an empty chain and recursively try adding each unused word that's a valid next step (i.e., the last word in the current chain is a subsequence of the candidate). At each step, we track the longest valid chain found. The algorithm works by: 1) Initializing a global variable to store the longest chain length. 2) Using a recursive backtracking function that takes the current chain and set of unused words. 3) For each unused word, if it's valid (the last word in chain is a subsequence of it, or chain is empty), add it to the chain, mark it used, and recurse. 4) After recursion, backtrack by removing the word and marking it unused. 5) Update the longest length when a chain ends. Time complexity is O(n! * L) where n is number of words and L is average word length, due to n! permutations and subsequence checks taking O(L). Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and used markers. Connection to L1: This uses the subsequence check from L1 as a helper. Common mistakes include not handling empty chains properly, forgetting to backtrack, or missing the base case to update the longest chain. Real code pattern: def longest_chain(words): max_len = 0; used = [False]*len(words); def backtrack(chain): nonlocal max_len; if len(chain) > max_len: max_len = len(chain); for i in range(len(words)): if not used[i] and (not chain or is_subsequence(chain[-1], words[i])): used[i] = True; chain.append(words[i]); backtrack(chain); chain.pop(); used[i] = False; backtrack([]); return max_len.

examples.trace: Start: max_len=0, used=[F,F,F], chain=[]. Call backtrack([]). Chain empty, try all words. i=0: word="a", valid (chain empty), used[0]=T, chain=["a"]. Recurse backtrack(["a"]). Check unused: i=1 word="ab", is_subsequence("a","ab")=True, used[1]=T, chain=["a","ab"]. Recurse backtrack(["a","ab"]). Check unused: i=2 word="abc", is_subsequence("ab","abc")=True, used[2]=T, chain=["a","ab","abc"]. Recurse backtrack(["a","ab","abc"]). Update max_len=3. Backtrack: chain.pop to ["a","ab"], used[2]=F. No more unused, backtrack: chain.pop to ["a"], used[1]=F. i=2 word="abc", is_subsequence("a","abc")=True, used[2]=T, chain=["a","abc"]. Recurse backtrack(["a","abc"]). Check unused: i=1 word="ab", is_subsequence("abc","ab")=False (since 'c' missing), skip. Update max_len=2. Backtrack: chain.pop to ["a"], used[2]=F. Backtrack: chain.pop to [], used[0]=F. i=1: word="ab", valid, used[1]=T, chain=["ab"]. Recurse backtrack(["ab"]). Check unused: i=0 word="a", is_subsequence("ab","a")=False, skip. i=2 word="abc", valid, used[2]=T, chain=["ab","abc"]. Recurse, update max_len=2. Backtrack similarly. i=2: word="abc", valid, chain=["abc"], recurse, max_len=1. End: max_len=3.

Start: max_len=0, used=[F,F,F], chain=[]. Call backtrack([]). i=0: word="x", valid, used[0]=T, chain=["x"]. Recurse backtrack(["x"]). Check unused: i=1 word="xy", is_subsequence("x","xy")=True, used[1]=T, chain=["x","xy"]. Recurse, update max_len=2. Check i=2 word="yx", is_subsequence("x","yx")=True, used[2]=T, chain=["x","yx"]. Recurse, update max_len=2. Backtrack. i=1: word="xy", valid, chain=["xy"]. Recurse: i=0 word="x", is_subsequence("xy","x")=False; i=2 word="yx", is_subsequence("xy","yx")=False (order mismatch). Update max_len=1. i=2: word="yx", valid, chain=["yx"]. Recurse: i=0 word="x", is_subsequence("yx","x")=True, chain=["yx","x"], update max_len=2; i=1 word="xy", is_subsequence("yx","xy")=False. End: max_len=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Checking if One String is a Subsequence of Another
ladders.desc: Determine if string A is a subsequence of string B by matching characters in order.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that for A to be a subsequence of B, all characters of A must appear in B in the same order, but not necessarily consecutively. This is a fundamental check for building valid chains. The algorithm uses two pointers: one for A (i) and one for B (j). Start both at 0. While i < len(A) and j < len(B), if A[i] equals B[j], increment i (move to next char in A). Always increment j (move through B). After the loop, if i equals len(A), all characters of A were found in order in B, so A is a subsequence of B. Time complexity is O(L) where L is length of B, as we traverse B once. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in backtracking to validate chain steps. Common mistakes include not resetting pointers correctly, checking for subsequence in wrong order, or using substring instead of subsequence logic. Real code pattern: def is_subsequence(a, b): i = 0; j = 0; while i < len(a) and j < len(b): if a[i] == b[j]: i += 1; j += 1; return i == len(a).

examples.trace: Start: i=0, j=0. a[0]='a', b[0]='a', match: i=1, j=1. i=1, j=1: a[1]='b', b[1]='b', match: i=2, j=2. i=2 equals len(a)=2, loop ends. Return True.
Start: i=0, j=0. a[0]='a', b[0]='a', match: i=1, j=1. i=1, j=1: a[1]='c', b[1]='b', no match: j=2. i=1, j=2: a[1]='c', b[2]='c', match: i=2, j=3. i=2 equals len(a)=2, return True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking to Explore All Permutations
ladders.desc: Use recursion with backtracking to generate all possible orderings of words without storing all permutations.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to find the longest chain, we need to test every sequence of words, but generating all permutations explicitly (n! of them) would be memory-intensive. Backtracking allows us to explore these permutations incrementally by building chains recursively and undoing choices (backtracking) to try alternatives. This reduces space usage to O(n) for recursion depth and a used array. The algorithm: 1) Maintain a current chain (list) and a boolean array 'used' to track which words are in the chain. 2) Define a recursive function that tries to extend the chain by adding each unused word that satisfies the subsequence condition (or if chain is empty). 3) After adding a word, recurse to explore further extensions. 4) Upon return, remove the word (backtrack) and mark it unused to try other words. 5) Keep a global variable to update the maximum chain length found. Time complexity is O(n! * L) in worst case as it explores all permutations, with L for subsequence checks. Space is O(n). Connection to L0: This is the main recursive framework of the full solution. Common mistakes include not copying the chain properly (should use append/pop), forgetting to update the max length, or infinite recursion due to missing base case. Real code pattern: def backtrack(chain, used): nonlocal max_len; max_len = max(max_len, len(chain)); for i in range(n): if not used[i] and (not chain or is_subsequence(chain[-1], words[i])): used[i] = True; chain.append(words[i]); backtrack(chain, used); chain.pop(); used[i] = False.

examples.trace: Initial call backtrack([], [F,F]). max_len updated to 0. Loop i=0: word="a", valid (chain empty), used[0]=T, chain=["a"]. Recurse backtrack(["a"], [T,F]). Update max_len=1. Loop: i=1 word="b", is_subsequence("a","b")=False, skip. Return. Backtrack: chain.pop to [], used[0]=F. i=1: word="b", valid, used[1]=T, chain=["b"]. Recurse backtrack(["b"], [F,T]). Update max_len=1. Loop: i=0 word="a", is_subsequence("b","a")=False. Return. Backtrack: chain.pop to [], used[1]=F. End.

backtrack([], [F,F]). max_len=0. i=0: word="x", valid, used[0]=T, chain=["x"]. Recurse backtrack(["x"], [T,F]). max_len=1. i=1: word="xy", is_subsequence("x","xy")=True, used[1]=T, chain=["x","xy"]. Recurse backtrack(["x","xy"], [T,T]). max_len=2. Return. Backtrack: chain.pop to ["x"], used[1]=F. Return. Backtrack: chain.pop to [], used[0]=F. i=1: word="xy", valid, used[1]=T, chain=["xy"]. Recurse backtrack(["xy"], [F,T]). max_len=2. i=0: word="x", is_subsequence("xy","x")=False. Return. Backtrack: chain.pop to [], used[1]=F. End.

testCases.input: words = ["x", "y", "z"], start chain = [], used = [F, F, F]
words = ["a", "aa", "aaa"], start chain = [], used = [F, F, F]
words = ["abc", "ab", "a"], start chain = [], used = [F, F, F]
words = ["a", "b", "ab"], start chain = [], used = [F, F, F]

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Function with State Management
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive function that maintains state (current chain and used words) to explore combinations.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that recursion allows us to break down the problem of exploring all chains into smaller subproblems: given a current chain, try all possible next words. State management is crucial to avoid revisiting words and to build chains incrementally. We use parameters: 'chain' (list of words so far) and 'used' (boolean list tracking included words). The recursive function: 1) Base case: update a global maximum if current chain length is greater. 2) Iterate over all words; for each unused word that can follow the last word in chain (or if chain is empty), add it to the chain, mark it used, and recurse. 3) After recursion, remove the word from chain and mark it unused (backtracking). This ensures all permutations are tried. Time complexity for this function in isolation depends on number of recursive calls, but in full context it's O(n! * L). Space is O(n) for recursion stack and used array. Connection to L2: This is the implementation of the backtracking logic. Common mistakes include using global variables incorrectly, not passing state by reference properly, or missing the backtracking step. Real code pattern: def explore(chain, used): global max_len; max_len = max(max_len, len(chain)); for i in range(len(words)): if not used[i] and (len(chain) == 0 or is_subsequence(chain[-1], words[i])): used[i] = True; chain.append(words[i]); explore(chain, used); chain.pop(); used[i] = False.

examples.trace: Call explore([], [F,F]). max_len updated to 0. Loop i=0: word="a", valid (chain empty), used[0]=T, chain=["a"]. Recurse explore(["a"], [T,F]). max_len updated to 1. Loop i=1: word="b", is_subsequence("a","b")=False, skip. Return. Backtrack: chain.pop to [], used[0]=F. i=1: word="b", valid, used[1]=T, chain=["b"]. Recurse explore(["b"], [F,T]). max_len stays 1. Loop i=0: word="a", is_subsequence("b","a")=False. Return. Backtrack: chain.pop to [], used[1]=F. End.

explore([], [F,F]). max_len=0. i=0: word="x", valid, used[0]=T, chain=["x"]. Recurse explore(["x"], [T,F]). max_len=1. i=1: word="xy", is_subsequence("x","xy")=True, used[1]=T, chain=["x","xy"]. Recurse explore(["x","xy"], [T,T]). max_len=2. Return. Backtrack: chain.pop to ["x"], used[1]=F. Return. Backtrack: chain.pop to [], used[0]=F. i=1: word="xy", valid, used[1]=T, chain=["xy"]. Recurse explore(["xy"], [F,T]). max_len=2. i=0: word="x", is_subsequence("xy","x")=False. Return. Backtrack: chain.pop to [], used[1]=F. End.

testCases.input: words = ["a", "b", "c"], chain = [], used = [F, F, F], max_len = 0
words = ["a", "aa", "aaa"], chain = [], used = [F, F, F], max_len = 0

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over a List with Condition Checking
ladders.desc: Loop through a list of words and apply a condition to select valid next words for a chain.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that at each step in building a chain, we need to consider all available words and pick those that can legally follow the current last word. This involves iterating over the list and checking a condition: if the chain is empty, any word is valid; otherwise, the last word must be a subsequence of the candidate word. This operation is fundamental to the backtracking process. The algorithm: 1) Given a list 'words', a current 'chain' (list), and a 'used' boolean array. 2) Use a for-loop to iterate over indices from 0 to len(words)-1. 3) For each index i, if used[i] is False, check if chain is empty or is_subsequence(chain[-1], words[i]) is True. 4) If true, this word is a candidate to extend the chain. Time complexity is O(n * L) per call due to iterating over n words and subsequence check taking O(L). Space is O(1) for the loop. Connection to L3: This is the core loop inside the recursive function. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop range, not checking the used array, or incorrect condition order (e.g., checking subsequence when chain is empty). Real code pattern: for i in range(len(words)): if not used[i] and (len(chain) == 0 or is_subsequence(chain[-1], words[i])): # valid candidate.

examples.trace: Loop i=0: word="a", used[0]=True, skip. i=1: word="b", used[1]=False, chain not empty, check is_subsequence("a","b"): a='a', b='b', no match, so False. i=2: word="ab", used[2]=False, check is_subsequence("a","ab"): match, so True. Thus only i=2 is valid.
Chain empty, so condition reduces to 'not used[i]'. Loop: i=0: used[0]=False, valid. i=1: used[1]=False, valid. i=2: used[2]=False, valid.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subsequences in Strings
ladders.desc: Learn what it means for one string to be a subsequence of another, with simple examples.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a subsequence is a sequence that can be derived from another sequence by deleting some or no elements without changing the order of the remaining elements. For strings, string A is a subsequence of string B if all characters of A appear in B in the same order, but they don't have to be consecutive. This is a key prerequisite for the word chain problem. To check if A is a subsequence of B, imagine two pointers: one scanning A and one scanning B. Move through B, and whenever you find a character matching the current character in A, advance the A pointer. If you reach the end of A, it's a subsequence. For example, "ace" is a subsequence of "abcde" because 'a', 'c', 'e' appear in order in "abcde". But "axe" is not because 'x' is not in "abcde". Time complexity to check is O(length of B). Space is O(1). Connection to L4: This condition is used in the iteration loop to filter valid words. Common mistakes include confusing subsequence with substring (which requires consecutive characters) or not preserving order. Real code pattern: Simple explanation: def is_subsequence(a, b): i = 0; for char in b: if i < len(a) and a[i] == char: i += 1; return i == len(a).

examples.trace: Check if "cat" is subsequence of "captain". Start i=0 for A. B: 'c' matches A[0]='c', i=1. B: 'a' matches A[1]='a', i=2. B: 'p' no match. B: 't' matches A[2]='t', i=3. i=3 equals len(A)=3, so True.
Check "bat" in "table". i=0: B 't' no match to 'b'. B 'a' no match. B 'b' matches A[0]='b', i=1. B 'l' no match to 'a'. B 'e' no match. End, i=1 not equal to 3, so False.

approaches.name: Sorting with DFS and Memoization O(n^2 * L)
approaches.summary: Sort words by length to ensure we only consider chains where each word is shorter or equal in length to the next. Use DFS with memoization to find the longest chain starting from each word, checking subsequence relationships.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Chain of Words Using Sorting with DFS and Memoization
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find the longest chain where each word is a subsequence of the next using sorting, DFS, and memoization.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that by sorting words by length, we ensure that when building chains, we only consider words that are shorter or equal in length to subsequent words, which is necessary for subsequence relationships. This reduces unnecessary comparisons. The core idea is to treat each word as a node in a directed graph where an edge exists from word A to word B if A is a subsequence of B and length(A) < length(B). The problem then reduces to finding the longest path in this DAG. We use DFS with memoization (dynamic programming) to compute the longest chain starting from each word, avoiding recomputation. The algorithm: 1. Sort words by length. 2. For each word, use DFS to find the longest chain starting from it. In DFS, check all longer words to see if the current word is a subsequence; if yes, recursively compute the chain length from that longer word. Memoize results to store the longest chain length starting from each word. 3. Track the maximum chain length found. Time complexity is O(n² * L) where n is the number of words and L is the average word length, due to sorting (O(n log n)) and subsequence checks (O(L) each) for up to n² pairs. Space complexity is O(n) for memoization and sorting. Connection to L1: This builds on the subsequence check helper function. Common mistakes include not sorting by length (leading to incorrect chain building), forgetting to handle equal-length words (they cannot form chains since subsequence requires shorter length), and not using memoization (causing exponential time). Real code pattern: def longestChain(words): words.sort(key=len); memo = {}; def dfs(i): if i in memo: return memo[i]; max_len = 1; for j in range(i+1, len(words)): if isSubsequence(words[i], words[j]): max_len = max(max_len, 1 + dfs(j)); memo[i] = max_len; return max_len; result = 0; for i in range(len(words)): result = max(result, dfs(i)); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort words by length: ["a", "b", "ba", "bca", "bda", "bdca"]. Step 2: Initialize memo = {}. Step 3: Start DFS from index 0 (word "a"): Check longer words: "b" (not subsequence), "ba" (subsequence: a in ba), so call dfs(2) for "ba". dfs(2): Check longer words: "bca" (subsequence: ba in bca), call dfs(3) for "bca". dfs(3): Check longer words: "bdca" (subsequence: bca in bdca), call dfs(5) for "bdca". dfs(5): No longer words, return 1. Back to dfs(3): max_len = max(1, 1+1) = 2, memo[3]=2, return 2. Back to dfs(2): max_len = max(1, 1+2) = 3, memo[2]=3, return 3. Back to dfs(0): max_len = max(1, 1+3) = 4, memo[0]=4, return 4. Step 4: Continue DFS from other indices, but max found is 4. Result: 4.

Step 1: Sort words by length: ["x", "y", "z", "xx", "yx", "zz"]. Step 2: Initialize memo = {}. Step 3: Start DFS from index 0 (word "x"): Check longer words: "xx" (subsequence: x in xx), call dfs(3) for "xx". dfs(3): No longer words, return 1. Back to dfs(0): max_len = max(1, 1+1) = 2, memo[0]=2, return 2. Step 4: DFS from index 1 ("y"): Check longer words: "yx" (subsequence: y in yx), call dfs(4) for "yx". dfs(4): No longer words, return 1. Back to dfs(1): max_len = max(1, 1+1) = 2, memo[1]=2, return 2. Step 5: DFS from other indices yield max 1. Overall max is 2. Result: 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Checking if One String is a Subsequence of Another
ladders.desc: Implement a function to determine if string A is a subsequence of string B.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a subsequence means all characters of A appear in B in the same order, but not necessarily consecutively. This is a fundamental check for building word chains. The algorithm uses a two-pointer technique: iterate through both strings, advancing the pointer in B when characters match, and only advancing in A when a match is found. If we reach the end of A, it's a subsequence; otherwise, it's not. Time complexity is O(L) where L is the length of B, as we may traverse all characters. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in DFS to check subsequence relationships between words. Common mistakes include checking for substrings instead of subsequences (which requires consecutive characters), or using inefficient methods like generating all subsequences (exponential time). Real code pattern: def isSubsequence(a, b): i, j = 0, 0; while i < len(a) and j < len(b): if a[i] == b[j]: i += 1; j += 1; else: j += 1; return i == len(a).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize i=0 (a[0]='b'), j=0 (b[0]='b'). Step 2: Match: a[0]='b' equals b[0]='b', so i=1, j=1. Step 3: Now i=1 (a[1]='a'), j=1 (b[1]='c'). No match, so j=2. Step 4: i=1 (a[1]='a'), j=2 (b[2]='a'). Match: i=2, j=3. Step 5: i=2 equals len(a)=2, so return True.

Step 1: Initialize i=0 (a[0]='a'), j=0 (b[0]='a'). Match: i=1, j=1. Step 2: i=1 (a[1]='b'), j=1 (b[1]='c'). No match, so j=2. Step 3: i=1 (a[1]='b'), j=2 (b[2]='d'). No match, j=3. Step 4: j=3 equals len(b)=3, loop ends. i=1 not equal to len(a)=3, so return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DFS with Memoization for Longest Path in a DAG
ladders.desc: Use DFS and memoization to find the longest path starting from each node in a directed acyclic graph.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that in a DAG, we can compute the longest path from each node by exploring all outgoing edges and caching results to avoid redundant calculations. This is a dynamic programming approach on graphs. The algorithm: For each node, perform DFS: if the node is memoized, return the cached value; otherwise, initialize max length as 1 (the node itself), then for each neighbor (nodes reachable via edges), recursively compute the longest path from that neighbor and update max length as 1 + that value. Memoize and return the max length. Time complexity is O(V+E) with memoization, where V is nodes and E is edges, as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for memoization and recursion stack. Connection to L0: This logic is applied to words treated as nodes, with edges based on subsequence checks. Common mistakes include not handling cycles (but here the graph is acyclic due to length sorting), forgetting to add 1 for the current node, or using BFS incorrectly (BFS is for shortest path). Real code pattern: def longestPath(graph, node, memo): if node in memo: return memo[node]; max_len = 1; for neighbor in graph[node]: max_len = max(max_len, 1 + longestPath(graph, neighbor, memo)); memo[node] = max_len; return max_len.

examples.trace: Step 1: Call longestPath(0). Not in memo. Step 2: max_len = 1. Check neighbors: neighbor 1: call longestPath(1). Not in memo. max_len for node 1: 1. Neighbors of 1: [3]. Call longestPath(3). Not in memo. max_len for node 3: 1 (no neighbors). memo[3]=1, return 1. Back to node 1: max_len = max(1, 1+1)=2. memo[1]=2, return 2. Back to node 0: update max_len = max(1, 1+2)=3. Step 3: Check neighbor 2: call longestPath(2). Not in memo. max_len for node 2: 1. Neighbors: [3]. Call longestPath(3): now in memo, return 1. Back to node 2: max_len = max(1, 1+1)=2. memo[2]=2, return 2. Back to node 0: max_len = max(3, 1+2)=3. memo[0]=3, return 3.

Step 1: Call longestPath(0). Not in memo. max_len = 1. Neighbor 1: call longestPath(1). Not in memo. max_len for node 1: 1. Neighbor 2: call longestPath(2). Not in memo. max_len for node 2: 1 (no neighbors). memo[2]=1, return 1. Back to node 1: max_len = max(1, 1+1)=2. memo[1]=2, return 2. Back to node 0: max_len = max(1, 1+2)=3. memo[0]=3, return 3.

testCases.expected: Cycle error (but assume acyclic for this problem)

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting Words by Length and Handling Equal Lengths
ladders.desc: Sort a list of words by their length and understand why equal-length words cannot form chains.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that for a word A to be a subsequence of word B, A must be shorter or equal in length to B. If lengths are equal, A can only be a subsequence of B if A equals B, but in a chain, we require distinct words, so equal-length words cannot form a chain step. Sorting by length ensures that when we iterate, we only compare each word with longer words, optimizing the process and enforcing the subsequence condition. The algorithm: Use a stable sort (e.g., by length) on the list of words. After sorting, equal-length words are grouped together, and we skip comparisons between them in DFS. Time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting, where n is the number of words. Space complexity is O(n) for the sorted list. Connection to L0: This preprocessing step is crucial for building the graph efficiently. Common mistakes include not sorting (which breaks the DFS logic), or including equal-length words in chain calculations (which would require them to be identical, violating distinctness). Real code pattern: words.sort(key=len) or sorted(words, key=len).

examples.trace: Step 1: List words with lengths: "bdca" (4), "bca" (3), "ba" (2), "a" (1), "b" (1), "bda" (3). Step 2: Sort by length ascending: lengths 1: "a", "b"; length 2: "ba"; length 3: "bca", "bda"; length 4: "bdca". Step 3: Within equal lengths, order may vary but doesn't affect chain logic. Sorted list: ["a", "b", "ba", "bca", "bda", "bdca"].
Step 1: Lengths: "xx" (2), "x" (1), "yy" (2), "y" (1). Step 2: Sort by length: length 1: "x", "y"; length 2: "xx", "yy". Step 3: Sorted list: ["x", "y", "xx", "yy"].

testCases.expected: ["z", "y", "x"] (all length 1, order may vary but sorted by length first)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Pairs of Words with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested loops to compare each word with all longer words in a sorted list.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to build chains, we need to check relationships between every pair of words where the first is shorter. After sorting by length, we can use nested loops: outer loop iterates over each word, and inner loop iterates over words that come after it (which are guaranteed to be longer or equal length). This ensures we only consider valid pairs for subsequence checks. The algorithm: For i from 0 to n-1, for j from i+1 to n-1, check if words[i] is a subsequence of words[j]. This generates up to n*(n-1)/2 pairs, but with sorting, we avoid pairs where words[i] is longer. Time complexity is O(n²) for the loops, plus O(L) for each subsequence check. Space complexity is O(1) for the loops. Connection to L0: This is how we implicitly build the graph edges in DFS. Common mistakes include iterating over all pairs without sorting (inefficient), or including j <= i (redundant). Real code pattern: for i in range(len(words)): for j in range(i+1, len(words)): if isSubsequence(words[i], words[j]): # process edge.

examples.trace: Step 1: n=3. Outer loop i=0 (word "a"): Inner loop j=1 ("ab"): check subsequence. j=2 ("abc"): check subsequence. Step 2: i=1 ("ab"): j=2 ("abc"): check subsequence. Step 3: i=2 ("abc"): no j (j starts at 3, out of range). Total pairs: 3.

examples.output: Pairs checked: none valid for chains since all equal length
examples.trace: Step 1: n=3. Outer loop i=0 ("x"): j=1 ("y"): lengths equal, skip in chain logic (but still could check subsequence, but it's false). j=2 ("z"): similar. Step 2: i=1 and i=2: similar. In practice, subsequence checks return False, so no edges formed.

testCases.input: words = ["a", "ab", "abc"], return list of pairs (i,j) checked
words = ["a", "b", "c"], return list of pairs checked
words = ["ab", "cd", "ef"], return list of pairs checked
words = ["a"], return list of pairs checked
words = ["a", "aa", "aaa"], return list of pairs checked
words = ["x", "xx", "xxx", "xxxx"], return list of pairs checked
words = ["cat", "dog", "fish"], return list of pairs checked
words = ["", "a", "ab"], return list of pairs checked
words = ["python", "java"], return list of pairs checked
words = ["a", "b", "ab", "bc"], return list of pairs checked

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subsequences in Strings
ladders.desc: Learn what a subsequence is and how it differs from a substring.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a subsequence of a string is a new string formed by deleting some characters (possibly none) from the original string without changing the order of the remaining characters. This is a fundamental concept in string processing. For example, "ace" is a subsequence of "abcde" because we can delete 'b' and 'd'. Key points: 1. Order must be preserved. 2. Characters need not be consecutive. 3. Every string is a subsequence of itself (by deleting zero characters). 4. The empty string is a subsequence of any string. Contrast with substring: a substring requires characters to be consecutive (e.g., "bcd" is a substring of "abcde"). Understanding this is crucial for the word chain problem because each word must be a subsequence of the next, allowing flexibility in chain formation. Connection to L0: This is the basic relationship that defines edges in the graph. Common mistakes include confusing subsequence with substring or anagram. Real code pattern: Explained in L1, but conceptually, check if all chars of A appear in B in order.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check 'a' in B: found at index 0. Step 2: Check 'b' in B after index 0: found at index 2. Step 3: Check 'c' in B after index 2: found at index 5. All characters found in order, so True.
Step 1: Check 'a' in B: found at index 0. Step 2: Check 'x' in B after index 0: not found (B has 'h','b','g','d','c'), so False.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Sorting O(n^2 * L)
approaches.summary: Sort words by length and use dynamic programming where dp[i] represents the longest chain ending at word i. For each word, iterate over previous shorter words to update dp[i] if a valid subsequence chain exists.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Chain of Words Using DP with Sorting
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find the longest chain where each word is a subsequence of the next using dynamic programming after sorting by length.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that by sorting words by length first, we ensure that when checking if a shorter word is a subsequence of a longer word, we only need to look backward in the sorted list. This ordering allows us to apply dynamic programming efficiently. The dp[i] array stores the length of the longest chain ending at word i. For each word i, we iterate over all previous words j (which are shorter or equal in length due to sorting) and check if word j is a subsequence of word i. If it is, we update dp[i] to be max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). The final answer is the maximum value in the dp array. The time complexity is O(n² * L) where n is the number of words and L is the average word length, due to the nested loops and subsequence checks. Space complexity is O(n) for the dp array. This connects to L1 by using the subsequence check as a helper function. Common mistakes include not handling empty word lists, forgetting to initialize dp values to 1 (each word is a chain of length 1), and incorrect subsequence logic. Real code pattern: 1. Sort words by length. 2. Initialize dp array with 1s. 3. For i from 0 to n-1: For j from 0 to i-1: If isSubsequence(words[j], words[i]): dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). 4. Return max(dp).

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort words by length: ["a", "b", "ba", "bca", "bda", "bdca"]. Step 2: Initialize dp = [1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1]. Step 3: For i=0 ("a"): no previous words, dp[0]=1. Step 4: For i=1 ("b"): j=0 ("a"), check if "a" is subsequence of "b"? No, dp[1]=1. Step 5: For i=2 ("ba"): j=0 ("a"), "a" is subsequence of "ba"? Yes, dp[2]=max(1, dp[0]+1=2)=2. j=1 ("b"), "b" is subsequence of "ba"? Yes, dp[2]=max(2, dp[1]+1=2)=2. Step 6: For i=3 ("bca"): j=0 ("a"), "a" is subsequence of "bca"? Yes, dp[3]=max(1, dp[0]+1=2)=2. j=1 ("b"), "b" is subsequence of "bca"? Yes, dp[3]=max(2, dp[1]+1=2)=2. j=2 ("ba"), "ba" is subsequence of "bca"? Yes (b and a in order), dp[3]=max(2, dp[2]+1=3)=3. Step 7: For i=4 ("bda"): j=0 ("a"), "a" is subsequence of "bda"? Yes, dp[4]=max(1, dp[0]+1=2)=2. j=1 ("b"), "b" is subsequence of "bda"? Yes, dp[4]=max(2, dp[1]+1=2)=2. j=2 ("ba"), "ba" is subsequence of "bda"? Yes, dp[4]=max(2, dp[2]+1=3)=3. j=3 ("bca"), "bca" is subsequence of "bda"? No, dp[4]=3. Step 8: For i=5 ("bdca"): j=0 ("a"), "a" is subsequence of "bdca"? Yes, dp[5]=max(1, dp[0]+1=2)=2. j=1 ("b"), "b" is subsequence of "bdca"? Yes, dp[5]=max(2, dp[1]+1=2)=2. j=2 ("ba"), "ba" is subsequence of "bdca"? Yes, dp[5]=max(2, dp[2]+1=3)=3. j=3 ("bca"), "bca" is subsequence of "bdca"? Yes, dp[5]=max(3, dp[3]+1=4)=4. j=4 ("bda"), "bda" is subsequence of "bdca"? Yes, dp[5]=max(4, dp[4]+1=4)=4. Step 9: max(dp) = 4.

Step 1: Sort words by length: ["x", "y", "z", "xx", "yx", "zz"]. Step 2: Initialize dp = [1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1]. Step 3: For i=0 ("x"): no previous, dp[0]=1. Step 4: For i=1 ("y"): j=0 ("x"), "x" is subsequence of "y"? No, dp[1]=1. Step 5: For i=2 ("z"): j=0 ("x"), no; j=1 ("y"), no; dp[2]=1. Step 6: For i=3 ("xx"): j=0 ("x"), "x" is subsequence of "xx"? Yes, dp[3]=max(1, dp[0]+1=2)=2. j=1 ("y"), no; j=2 ("z"), no; dp[3]=2. Step 7: For i=4 ("yx"): j=0 ("x"), "x" is subsequence of "yx"? Yes, dp[4]=max(1, dp[0]+1=2)=2. j=1 ("y"), "y" is subsequence of "yx"? Yes, dp[4]=max(2, dp[1]+1=2)=2. j=2 ("z"), no; j=3 ("xx"), "xx" is subsequence of "yx"? No; dp[4]=2. Step 8: For i=5 ("zz"): j=0 ("x"), no; j=1 ("y"), no; j=2 ("z"), "z" is subsequence of "zz"? Yes, dp[5]=max(1, dp[2]+1=2)=2. j=3 ("xx"), no; j=4 ("yx"), no; dp[5]=2. Step 9: max(dp) = 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Checking if One String is a Subsequence of Another
ladders.desc: Implement a function to determine if string A is a subsequence of string B.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a subsequence maintains the order of characters but not necessarily continuity. We can check this by iterating through string B and matching characters of A in order. If we find all characters of A in B in the same sequence, A is a subsequence of B. The algorithm uses two pointers: one for A (i) and one for B (j). Start both at 0. While i < len(A) and j < len(B): if A[i] == B[j], increment i (move to next character in A). Always increment j (move through B). After the loop, if i equals len(A), all characters of A were found in order in B, so return true; otherwise, false. Time complexity is O(L) where L is the length of B, as we traverse B once. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the helper function used in the DP update step. Common mistakes include not resetting pointers for each check, incorrect loop conditions, or assuming subsequence requires contiguous characters. Real code pattern: def isSubsequence(a, b): i, j = 0, 0; while i < len(a) and j < len(b): if a[i] == b[j]: i += 1; j += 1; return i == len(a).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize i=0 (a="ba"), j=0 (b="bca"). Step 2: Check a[0]='b' vs b[0]='b': match, so i=1, j=1. Step 3: Check a[1]='a' vs b[1]='c': no match, j=2. Step 4: Check a[1]='a' vs b[2]='a': match, so i=2, j=3. Step 5: i=2 equals len(a)=2, so return true.

Step 1: i=0, j=0. a[0]='a' vs b[0]='a': match, i=1, j=1. Step 2: a[1]='b' vs b[1]='c': no match, j=2. Step 3: a[1]='b' vs b[2]='b': match, i=2, j=3. Step 4: i=2 < len(a)=3, but j=3 equals len(b)=3, loop ends. Step 5: i=2 != len(a)=3, so return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dynamic Programming for Chain Length Updates
ladders.desc: Apply DP to compute the longest chain length by updating dp[i] based on previous words.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that the longest chain ending at a word can be built by extending chains from shorter words that are subsequences of it. After sorting words by length, we use a dp array where dp[i] is the length of the longest chain ending at word i. Initialize all dp[i] to 1, as each word alone forms a chain of length 1. For each word i, iterate over all previous words j (0 to i-1). If word j is a subsequence of word i (checked using the L1 function), then we can extend the chain ending at word j by adding word i, so dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). The final answer is the maximum value in dp. This works because sorting ensures we only check shorter words, preventing cycles. Time complexity is O(n²) for the nested loops, plus O(n² * L) for subsequence checks in L0 context. Space complexity is O(n) for dp. This connects to L0 as the main DP logic. Common mistakes include not initializing dp to 1, updating dp[i] incorrectly, or missing the max operation. Real code pattern: dp = [1] * n; for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if isSubsequence(words[j], words[i]): dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1); return max(dp).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize dp = [1, 1, 1]. Step 2: For i=0 ("a"): no previous, dp[0]=1. Step 3: For i=1 ("ab"): j=0 ("a"), "a" is subsequence of "ab"? Yes, dp[1] = max(1, dp[0]+1=2) = 2. Step 4: For i=2 ("abc"): j=0 ("a"), "a" is subsequence of "abc"? Yes, dp[2] = max(1, dp[0]+1=2) = 2. j=1 ("ab"), "ab" is subsequence of "abc"? Yes, dp[2] = max(2, dp[1]+1=3) = 3. Step 5: max(dp) = 3.

Step 1: dp = [1, 1, 1]. Step 2: i=0: dp[0]=1. Step 3: i=1 ("y"): j=0 ("x"), "x" is subsequence of "y"? No, dp[1]=1. Step 4: i=2 ("xy"): j=0 ("x"), "x" is subsequence of "xy"? Yes, dp[2] = max(1, dp[0]+1=2) = 2. j=1 ("y"), "y" is subsequence of "xy"? Yes, dp[2] = max(2, dp[1]+1=2) = 2. Step 5: max(dp) = 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting Words by Length for DP Efficiency
ladders.desc: Sort an array of strings by their length to enable efficient DP chain building.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that in a chain where each word is a subsequence of the next, the words must be non-decreasing in length (since a subsequence cannot be longer than the string it's part of). By sorting words by length, we ensure that when processing word i, all potential predecessor words j (with j < i) are shorter or equal in length. This ordering prevents checking longer words as predecessors, which would be invalid, and simplifies the DP update to a single pass. The algorithm sorts the words array using a stable sort by the length of each string. After sorting, we can apply DP as in L2. Time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting, which is efficient. Space complexity is O(1) extra space if sorting in-place, or O(n) if creating a new array. This connects to L2 by providing the sorted input for DP. Common mistakes include not handling equal lengths correctly (sort should be stable but order doesn't matter for subsequence checks), or sorting alphabetically instead of by length. Real code pattern: words.sort(key=len) or sorted(words, key=len).

examples.trace: Step 1: List words with lengths: "bdca"=4, "ba"=2, "bca"=3, "a"=1, "b"=1. Step 2: Sort by length ascending: lengths 1,1,2,3,4. Step 3: For length 1: "a" and "b" (order may vary, but stable sort keeps original order if specified). Step 4: For length 2: "ba". Step 5: For length 3: "bca". Step 6: For length 4: "bdca". Result: ["a", "b", "ba", "bca", "bdca"].

Step 1: Lengths: "xyz"=3, "x"=1, "xy"=2, "xz"=2. Step 2: Sort by length: 1,2,2,3. Step 3: Length 1: "x". Step 4: Length 2: "xy" and "xz" (original order if stable). Step 5: Length 3: "xyz". Result: ["x", "xy", "xz", "xyz"].

testCases.expected: ["dog", "cat", "bird"] or any order as lengths are equal

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Arrays with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested loops to compare each element with all previous elements in an array.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to find relationships between elements (like chain updates in DP), we often need to compare each element with others. Nested loops allow this by having an outer loop iterate through each element, and an inner loop iterate through previous elements. In this context, for an array of size n, the outer loop index i goes from 0 to n-1, and the inner loop index j goes from 0 to i-1. This ensures we compare element i with all elements before it, without duplicates or missing pairs. The time complexity is O(n²) for the nested loops. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. This connects to L3 by being the mechanism to process sorted words in DP. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop ranges, using the same index for both loops, or not optimizing when possible. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): for j in range(i): # compare arr[j] and arr[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: n=4. Step 2: i=1: j from 0 to 0: print (0,1). Step 3: i=2: j from 0 to 1: print (0,2), (1,2). Step 4: i=3: j from 0 to 2: print (0,3), (1,3), (2,3).

examples.input: arr = ["a", "b", "c"], operation: count if arr[j] < arr[i] alphabetically
examples.trace: Step 1: n=3. Step 2: i=1 ("b"): j=0 ("a"), "a" < "b"? Yes, count=1. Step 3: i=2 ("c"): j=0 ("a"), "a" < "c"? Yes, count=2. j=1 ("b"), "b" < "c"? Yes, count=3. Total count=3.

testCases.input: arr = [], operation: sum of all pairs (j + i)
arr = [5], operation: sum of all pairs
arr = [1, 2], operation: product of all pairs (j * i)
arr = [3, 1, 4], operation: count pairs where arr[j] < arr[i]
arr = [10, 20, 30, 40], operation: average of all pairs ( (j+i)/2 )
arr = [true, false, true], operation: logical AND of all pairs
arr = ["cat", "dog", "fish"], operation: length sum of all pairs
arr = [0, 0, 0], operation: count pairs where arr[j] == arr[i]
arr = ["a", "b", "c", "d"], operation: list all pairs as tuples

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Loops
ladders.desc: Learn to store multiple items in an array and access them using loops.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that arrays are data structures that hold a collection of items (like strings or numbers) in order, allowing easy access by index. Loops are control structures that repeat a block of code, making it efficient to process each element in an array. For example, an array 'words' might contain strings ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]. We can use a for loop to iterate through each word: for i in range(len(words)): print(words[i]). This prints each word. Arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0. Loops can be nested to compare elements, as in higher levels. Time complexity for a single loop is O(n), where n is the array size. Space complexity is O(n) for the array storage. This connects to L4 as the foundation for nested loops. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using <= instead of <), confusing indices with values, or not initializing arrays properly. Real code pattern: arr = ["item1", "item2"]; for i in range(len(arr)): print(arr[i]).

examples.trace: Step 1: arr has 3 elements. Step 2: i=0: print arr[0]="red". Step 3: i=1: print arr[1]="green". Step 4: i=2: print arr[2]="blue".
Step 1: Initialize sum=0. Step 2: i=0: sum += arr[0]=1, sum=1. Step 3: i=1: sum += arr[1]=2, sum=3. Step 4: i=2: sum += arr[2]=3, sum=6. Step 5: i=3: sum += arr[3]=4, sum=10.

approaches.name: Optimized DP with HashMap O(n * L^2)
approaches.summary: Use a hashmap to store the longest chain length for each word. For each word, generate all possible predecessor words by removing one character and update the chain length in the hashmap, then take the maximum.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Chain of Words using Optimized DP with HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find the longest chain where each word is a subsequence of the next using DP with hashmap optimization.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires finding the longest chain of words where each word is a subsequence of the next. A subsequence means characters appear in the same order but not necessarily consecutively. The optimized DP approach uses a hashmap to store the longest chain length ending at each word. The intuition is to process words in increasing length order, as a shorter word can only be a subsequence of a longer word. For each word, we generate all possible predecessor words by removing exactly one character. For each predecessor, if it exists in our hashmap, we update the current word's chain length to be max(current, predecessor's chain length + 1). This works because if predecessor is a subsequence of current word, then any chain ending at predecessor can be extended by adding the current word. Sorting by length ensures we process words in correct dependency order. Time complexity is O(n * L^2) where n is number of words and L is max word length: O(n log n) for sorting, O(n * L) to generate predecessors (L possibilities each taking O(L) to check existence). Space is O(n) for hashmap. Connection to L1: This uses the predecessor generation subroutine from L1. Common mistakes: not sorting by length first (breaks DP dependencies), not handling duplicate words correctly, inefficient predecessor generation. Real code pattern: sort words by length, initialize dp hashmap with each word having length 1, for each word generate predecessors, update dp, track maximum.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Sort by length: ["a", "b", "ba", "bca", "bda", "bdca"]
Step 2: Initialize dp: {"a":1, "b":1, "ba":1, "bca":1, "bda":1, "bdca":1}
Step 3: Process "a": length 1, no predecessors (remove 1 char gives "" which doesn't exist). dp["a"] stays 1.
Step 4: Process "b": similar, dp["b"] stays 1.
Step 5: Process "ba": predecessors by removing 1 char: "a" and "b". Both exist. dp["ba"] = max(1, dp["a"]+1=2, dp["b"]+1=2) = 2.
Step 6: Process "bca": predecessors: "ba", "bc", "ca". Only "ba" exists. dp["bca"] = max(1, dp["ba"]+1=3) = 3.
Step 7: Process "bda": predecessors: "ba", "bd", "da". Only "ba" exists. dp["bda"] = max(1, dp["ba"]+1=3) = 3.
Step 8: Process "bdca": predecessors: "bca", "bda", "bdc", "bca", "dca", "bda", "bdc", "bca", "bda", "bdc". Unique: "bca", "bda", "bdc", "dca". "bca" and "bda" exist. dp["bdca"] = max(1, dp["bca"]+1=4, dp["bda"]+1=4) = 4.
Step 9: Maximum in dp is 4.

Step 1: Sort by length: ["x", "y", "z", "xx", "yx", "zz"]
Step 2: Initialize dp: all 1.
Step 3: Process "x": no predecessors, dp["x"]=1.
Step 4: Process "y": dp["y"]=1.
Step 5: Process "z": dp["z"]=1.
Step 6: Process "xx": predecessors: "x" exists. dp["xx"] = max(1, dp["x"]+1=2) = 2.
Step 7: Process "yx": predecessors: "y", "x". Both exist. dp["yx"] = max(1, dp["y"]+1=2, dp["x"]+1=2) = 2.
Step 8: Process "zz": predecessors: "z" exists. dp["zz"] = max(1, dp["z"]+1=2) = 2.
Step 9: Maximum in dp is 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Predecessor Words by Removing One Character
ladders.desc: Generate all possible words that can be obtained by removing exactly one character from a given word.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is crucial for the DP approach because we need to find all words that could be immediate predecessors in the chain. Given a word w of length L, a predecessor is any word obtained by removing exactly one character from w. The intuition is that if word A is a subsequence of word B, then A can be obtained from B by removing some characters (possibly zero). For immediate chain connection where A is directly before B, we consider removing exactly one character from B to get A. This ensures we only check valid predecessor relationships. Algorithm: For each position i from 0 to L-1, create a new string by concatenating w[0:i] and w[i+1:L]. Collect all these strings in a set to avoid duplicates. Time complexity: O(L^2) because we create L strings, each of length L-1, so total operations O(L*(L-1)) = O(L^2). Space: O(L) for storing the generated strings temporarily. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each word in L0 to find potential predecessors. Common mistakes: generating duplicates when characters repeat, inefficient string concatenation. Real code pattern: predecessors = set(), for i in range(len(word)): pred = word[:i] + word[i+1:], add to set.

examples.trace:
Step 1: L=3, positions 0,1,2.
Step 2: Remove char at 0: word[1:] = "bc", result "bc".
Step 3: Remove char at 1: word[:1]="a", word[2:]="c", concatenate "ac".
Step 4: Remove char at 2: word[:2]="ab", result "ab".
Step 5: Set contains "ab", "ac", "bc".

Step 1: L=3, positions 0,1,2.
Step 2: Remove char at 0: "aa".
Step 3: Remove char at 1: "ba".
Step 4: Remove char at 2: "ba".
Step 5: Set contains "aa", "ba" (duplicate removed).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dynamic Programming with HashMap for Chain Length
ladders.desc: Use DP with hashmap to store and update the longest chain length ending at each word.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is a dynamic programming approach where dp[word] represents the length of the longest chain ending with that word. The recurrence is: dp[word] = 1 + max(dp[predecessor]) over all predecessors of word that exist in the word list. The intuition is that to form the longest chain ending at word, we look at all words that can immediately precede it (i.e., are subsequences obtained by removing one character), and extend their chains by 1. We use a hashmap for O(1) lookup of dp values. The algorithm processes words in increasing length order to ensure dependencies are resolved. For each word, initialize dp[word]=1, generate predecessors, for each predecessor that exists in dp, update dp[word] = max(dp[word], dp[predecessor]+1). Time complexity per word is O(L^2) for predecessor generation and O(L) for lookups, so O(n * L^2) total. Space is O(n) for dp hashmap. Connection to L0: This is the main DP logic in L0. Connection to L1: Uses predecessor generation from L1. Common mistakes: not initializing dp values to 1, not processing in length order, updating dp incorrectly. Real code pattern: dp = {}, sort words by length, for word in words: dp[word]=1, for pred in generate_predecessors(word): if pred in dp: dp[word]=max(dp[word], dp[pred]+1).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Sort: ["a", "ab", "abc"].
Step 2: Process "a": dp["a"]=1, no predecessors.
Step 3: Process "ab": dp["ab"]=1, predecessors: "a" exists, dp["ab"]=max(1, dp["a"]+1=2)=2.
Step 4: Process "abc": dp["abc"]=1, predecessors: "ab" exists, dp["abc"]=max(1, dp["ab"]+1=3)=3.

Step 1: Sort: ["x", "y", "xy"].
Step 2: Process "x": dp["x"]=1.
Step 3: Process "y": dp["y"]=1.
Step 4: Process "xy": dp["xy"]=1, predecessors: "x" and "y" exist, dp["xy"]=max(1, dp["x"]+1=2, dp["y"]+1=2)=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking if One String is a Subsequence of Another
ladders.desc: Determine if string A is a subsequence of string B.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is foundational because the chain condition requires each word to be a subsequence of the next. A string A is a subsequence of string B if all characters of A appear in B in the same order, but not necessarily consecutively. The intuition is to traverse both strings with two pointers: pointer i for A and j for B. Move j through B, and whenever B[j] matches A[i], advance i. If i reaches the end of A, then A is a subsequence of B. Algorithm: Initialize i=0, for j from 0 to len(B)-1: if i < len(A) and A[i] == B[j]: i++. After loop, return i == len(A). Time complexity: O(L) where L is length of B. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: In L2, we implicitly use this when generating predecessors—if A is obtained by removing one char from B, then A is a subsequence of B. Common mistakes: not checking order correctly, off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: i=0, for char in B: if i < len(A) and A[i]==char: i+=1, return i==len(A).

examples.trace:
Step 1: i=0, j=0: B[0]='a' matches A[0]='a', i=1.
Step 2: j=1: B[1]='b' matches A[1]='b', i=2.
Step 3: i=2 equals len(A)=2, return true.

Step 1: i=0, j=0: B[0]='a' matches A[0]='a', i=1.
Step 2: j=1: B[1]='b' does not match A[1]='c', i stays 1.
Step 3: j=2: B[2]='c' matches A[1]='c', i=2.
Step 4: i=2 equals len(A)=2, return true.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Manipulation: Removing a Character at Index
ladders.desc: Create a new string by removing one character at a specified index from an original string.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used repeatedly in generating predecessor words. Given a string s and an index i (0-based), we need to create a new string that contains all characters of s except the one at position i. The intuition is to split the string into two parts: the prefix before index i, and the suffix after index i, then concatenate them. Algorithm: If i is 0, return s[1:]; if i is len(s)-1, return s[:-1]; otherwise return s[:i] + s[i+1:]. Time complexity: O(L) where L is length of s, because creating a new string of length L-1 takes linear time. Space: O(L) for the new string. Connection to L3: This operation is used to generate strings that are subsequences by removing one character. Connection to L1: This is the core step in L1's predecessor generation. Common mistakes: index out of bounds, inefficient concatenation in loops. Real code pattern: if i==0: return s[1:]; elif i==len(s)-1: return s[:-1]; else: return s[:i] + s[i+1:].

examples.trace:
Step 1: s = "abc", i=1.
Step 2: Prefix s[:1] = "a", suffix s[2:] = "c".
Step 3: Concatenate "a" + "c" = "ac".

Step 1: s = "hello", i=0.
Step 2: Since i=0, return s[1:] = "ello".

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings and Character Access
ladders.desc: Basic operations on strings: accessing characters by index and understanding string immutability.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept. A string is a sequence of characters. In programming, strings are typically immutable, meaning they cannot be changed after creation. We can access individual characters using zero-based indexing: s[0] gives the first character, s[1] the second, etc. The length of a string s is given by len(s). Substrings can be obtained using slicing: s[start:end] returns characters from index start to end-1. If start is omitted, it defaults to 0; if end is omitted, it defaults to len(s). The intuition is that strings are like arrays of characters, but operations that modify them create new strings. This is foundational because all higher-level operations (removing characters, checking subsequences) rely on accessing and manipulating these characters. Time complexity: accessing by index is O(1). Space: strings occupy O(L) space. Connection to L4: This knowledge is used to remove characters by accessing parts of the string. Common mistakes: confusing zero-based indexing, trying to modify strings in place. Real code pattern: s = "hello", first_char = s[0], last_char = s[-1], substring = s[1:4].

examples.trace:
Step 1: s = "apple".
Step 2: s[0] is 'a' (first character).
Step 3: s[1:4] gives characters from index 1 to 3: 'p', 'p', 'l' -> "ppl".

Step 1: s = "cat".
Step 2: len(s) = 3.
Step 3: s[-1] gives last character 't'.
